coredata.dc:title: Rapid and low-temperature synthesis of N, P co-doped yellow emitting carbon dots and their applications as antibacterial agent and detection probe to Sudan Red I
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Zhang, Zhixia
Liu, Xuemei
Zhang, Rui
Xiao, Xincai
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of long-wavelength emitting carbon dots (CDs) with good antibacterial function and biosensing ability through element doping method is the hotspot of present researches. This paper reports the rapid synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped long wavelength-emitting CDs (N, P-CDs, λem = 530 nm) under low reaction temperature (30 min, 60 °C). This method used glucose, polyethyleneimine and phosphoric acid as raw materials, and each raw material worked multi-functionally during the synthesis process. The impacts of raw materials and reaction parameters upon optical properties of prepared N, P-CDs have been studied, and the synthesis mechanism has been discussed. Meanwhile, N, P-CDs were used as antibacterial material with minimum inhibitory concentrations against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli at 7.5 μg·mL−1 and 0.5 mg·mL−1 respectively. The prepared CDs were also used to realize fluorescence imaging of S. aureus, and work as fluorescence probe to detect Sudan Red I with limit of detection at 43 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-emitting carbon dots
Rapid synthesis under low temperature
Antibacterial
Sudan Red I
Detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical properties of N, P-CDs
The detection of Sudan Red I

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption (blue line) and fluorescence emission spectra (black line) of N, P-CDs (b) The emission spectra of N, P-CDs obtained under different excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500 nm (The concentration of N, P-CDs was 1.8 mg·mL−1). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM image of the as-prepared N, P-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan, (b) O 1s XPS, (c) N 1s XPS, (d) C 1s XPS, (e) P 2p XPS of N, P-CDs. (f) The FTIR spectrum of N, P-CDs.
PL emission spectra of N, P-CDs with different (a) carbon source (b) phosphorus source and (c) nitrogen source.
SEM images of S. aureus treated with N, P-CDs for 0 h (a), 2 h (b), 4 h (c), 6 h (d), respectively.

Confocal laser microscopic images after incubation S. aureus with N, P-CDs at 37 °C for 1, 2, 3 h at excitation wavelengths, (a, d, g) 405 nm (blue), (b, e, h) 488 nm (green), and (c, f, i) 561 nm (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The impacts of common interference reagents to the fluorescence of N, P-CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs quenched by Sudan Red I. ([Sudan Red I] = 0, 0.043, 0.42, 0.83, 1.25, 2.17, 3.48, 6.52, 12.17, 17.39, 30.43, 36.95, 43.48, 52.17 × 10−6 mol·L−1). Inset: the standard curve of the quenching ratio of Sudan Red I at different concentrations to the fluorescence of N, P-CDs (The concentration of N, P-CDs is 0.3 mg·mL−1).
The overlap between absorption spectrum of Sudan Red I and the emission spectrum of N, P-CDs (The concentration of N, P-CDs is 1.8 mg·mL−1, and the concentration of Sudan Red I is 5 × 10−5 mol·L−1).
Analytical results for the detection of Sudan Red I in real sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rapid and low-temperature synthesis of N, P co-doped yellow emitting carbon dots and their applications as antibacterial agent and detection probe to Sudan Red I

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of long-wavelength emitting carbon dots (CDs) with good antibacterial function and biosensing ability through element doping method is the hotspot of present researches. This paper reports the rapid synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped long wavelength-emitting CDs (N, P-CDs, λem = 530 nm) under low reaction temperature (30 min, 60 °C). This method used glucose, polyethyleneimine and phosphoric acid as raw materials, and each raw material worked multi-functionally during the synthesis process. The impacts of raw materials and reaction parameters upon optical properties of prepared N, P-CDs have been studied, and the synthesis mechanism has been discussed. Meanwhile, N, P-CDs were used as antibacterial material with minimum inhibitory concentrations against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli at 7.5 μg·mL−1 and 0.5 mg·mL−1 respectively. The prepared CDs were also used to realize fluorescence imaging of S. aureus, and work as fluorescence probe to detect Sudan Red I with limit of detection at 43 nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their excellent optical properties, chemical stability and biological compatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in the fields like biochemical detection [1], optical device [2], biological imaging [3], drug delivery [4], and disease treatment [5]. The element doping in CDs [6–14] is the new-rising area of nanomaterial science and has attracted increasing interests from scientists because the doping of elements like nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus and boron could effectively adjust the electronic energy level structures and some local chemical properties of prepared CDs [6], changing CDs into functional nanomaterials. Among these elements, the nitrogen could strongly combine with carbon atom because of its five valence electrons [7]. Phosphorus can works as n-type donor to change the electrons and the optical properties of CDs [8]. Therefore, the doping of N and P onto CDs would make more active sites and better optical properties for prepared CDs [9,12–15].

At present, researchers have chosen lots of materials as N and P sources for N, P co-doping CDs, including natural plant extracts [9], small biological molecules [6,10], aniline compounds [11–13], ammonia (NH3·H2O) [14], ethylenediamine (EDA) [15–17] and polyethyleneimine (PEI) [18] as nitrogen sources, and ammonium phosphate [19,20], phosphate [10], adenosine phosphate [10,21], and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) [9,11–13,15,17,18] as phosphorus sources. Wide range of options in raw materials make prepared N, P-CDs vary greatly in their properties. For instance, with sodium citrate as a carbon source, diammonium phosphate as both nitrogen and phosphorus source, Xu et al. [19] prepared blue-emitting N, P-CDs (170 °C, 6 h) with a quantum yield (QY) at 53%. Liu and his team acquired green-emitting N, P-CDs (QY at 9.6%) with glucose (Glc) as a carbon source, EDA as a nitrogen source and H3PO4 as a phosphorus source [15]. Zhao et al. prepared red-emitting N, P-CDs with o-phenylenediamine as both carbon and nitrogen source, and concentrated H3PO4 as phosphorus source (5 h, 160 °C, QY of CDs at 15%) [13]. Although the selection of raw materials and the optimization of synthesis conditions both determine the acquisition of high-quality N, P-CDs [17,19,22,23], most of studies focused on the optimization of synthesis conditions, including reaction time, temperature and raw material ratio [19,23]. The synthesis mechanism, especially the impacts of raw materials upon the properties of product [17,22], needs to be further explored, to reduce the randomness in raw material selection.

Bacterial infection, especially those caused by drug-resistant bacteria, has always been a severe problem in clinical treatment. Because of excellent biocompatibility, CDs have been regarded as ideal “candidate” for antibacterial materials [24–29]. Doped CDs, such as S-CDs [27], N-CDs [25,28], and Ga-CDs [29] have been discovered to have good antibacterial abilities. Travlou N. A. found that the prepared N-CDs had stronger antibacterial ability than S-CDs under the same synthesis conditions [27]. The preparation of multi-functional CDs with both bacterial imaging ability and antibacterial ability are still a major challenge.

Sudan Red I is a kind of azo organic dye with at least one-N=N- structure [30], and has been widely used in textile, printing, cosmetics and food production [31]. However, the genotoxicity, mutagenicity and carcinogenicity [32] make it a potential threat to human health, so a simple and sensitive detection method to Sudan Red I becomes the target of researchers. Although CDs have used in the detection of metal ions [33,34], pesticides [35,36], drugs [15,37] and intracellular substance [35], the employment of N, P-CDs as probe to Sudan Red I has not been seen in published literatures.

This paper reports the rapid synthesis of high-quality yellow-emitting N, P-CDs (λem = 530 nm) under low temperature with Glc as carbon source, PEI as nitrogen source and H3PO4 as both phosphorus source and dehydrating agent. The impacts of raw materials towards prepared CDs have been explored, and the CDs have been characterized by multiple means. The mechanisms of synthesis and luminescence are also discussed. N, P-CDs can be used as antibacterial material, with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) at 7.5 μg·mL−1 and that against Escherichia coli (E. coli) at 0.5 mg·mL−1. CDs were also used in cell imaging of S. aureus. Furthermore, prepared N, P-CDs were used as a fluorescence probe to realize the sensitive detection of Sudan Red I in aqueous phase.

PEI1800 (≥99.0%) and polyphosphoric acid(PPA) (≥85.0%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Glc (≥99.5%), H3PO4 (≥85.0%), EDA (≥99.0%), hexanoic acid (≥98.0%), soluble starch from potato (AR) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tris-Base (Tris) (≥99.0%) was bought from ANGUS Chemical Company Co., Ltd. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 -diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl] ethanesulfonic acid cell culture reagents (HEPES) were purchased from Gibco (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Sijiqing Company (Hangzhou, China). S. aureus (CCTCC AB 91093), E. coli (CCTCC AB 93154), Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae, CCTCC AY 92003), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, CCTCC AB 2013184) and Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris, CCTCC AB 91103) were provided by China Center for Type Culture Collection.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a LS55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer Company). UV–visible absorption spectra were acquired with a Lambda-35 UV–visible Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Company). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). TEM image was obtained with a JEM2100F transmission electron microscope (Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Company). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectra were obtained on VG Multilab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Electron Corporation). Images of S. aureus were captured using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss SIGMA). The zeta potentials of N, P-CDs were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). A fluorescence optical microscope (Nikon ECLIPSE Ti) was used for capturing the images of the laser-scanning confocal fluorescence of bacterial cells. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

To prepare N, P-CDs,1.5 g of PEI1800 and 0.2 g of Glc were dispersed in 1 mL of deionized (DI) water, and 2 mL of H3PO4 was added. The solution was heated at 60 °C for 0.5 h until the products turn to brown. DI water was added to the prepared N, P-CDs for dispersion and dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis membrane for 24 h. The product was then freeze-dried to obtain the amorphous N, P-CDs. When investigating the influence of raw material selection on N, P-CDs, the reagents instead of carbon source, nitrogen source or phosphorus source all use the same concentration as the original reaction.

Using rhodamine 6G (refractive index η = 1.36) dissolved in ethanol as a reference, the relative QY of N, P-CDs was calculated. N, P-CDs need to be dissolved in water (η = 1.33), and the UV absorption at 420 nm is less than 0.1. The formula for calculating relative QY is as follows,
where Ф is QY, Grad is the ratio of fluorescence area to absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent, ST is the standard substance, and x is the analyte.

S. aureus was cultured in Broth medium and E. coli was cultured in Luria Broth (LB) medium. Bacteria were grown at 37 °C with shaking at 180 rpm. The concentration of bacteria was determined by measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600) via UV–vis spectroscopy.

The MIC of N, P-CDs was determined by microdilution using a 96-well cell culture plate. Bacteria were adjusted to optical density of 2 × 106 CFU·mL−1. Each well contain 50 μL bacterial and 50 μL various contentions of N, P-CDs. The experiment was repeated three times.

S. aureus (≈109 CFU/mL, 950 μL) was incubated with 50 μL of N, P-CDs (10 mg·mL−1) for 2, 4, 6 h, respectively. S. aureus treated without N, P-CDs was used as blank controls. The cells were obtained by high speed centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and the substrate was removed with DI water. The cells were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 12 h at 4 °C. Then, the bacterial cells were sequentially dehydrated with alcohol solutions (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90% and 100%) for 10 min for each step. Next, it was dried by vacuum and sputter coated with gold. The samples were observed under a Zeiss SIGMA FESEM.

In the experiment, S. aureus was used as a typical target bacterium. Bacterial cells were cultured in a medium at 37 °C for 12 h. 1 mL of the culture suspension was taken and centrifuged at 1800 rpm for 5 min, then was washed twice with HEPES buffer (pH = 7.4) to remove the suspension, and the pellet was suspended in 950 μL of HEPES buffer. Subsequently, 50 μL of N, P-CDs solution (10 mg·mL−1) was added to solutions containing S. aureus and cultured for 1, 2, 3 h, respectively. Finally, bacterial cells were observed with a confocal fluorescence microscope.

The MTT method was used to detect the cell viability of N, P-CDs. HepG2 cells were first seeded in 100 mL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 12% FBS on 96-well plates and cultured at 5.0% CO2 and 37 °C for 24 h to ensure that the cells adhered to the upper surface of the plates. Then, the culture medium in each well was replaced with the same volume of DMEM medium in the presence of N, P-CDs at different concentrations (10–150 μg/mL). Cells not treated with N, P-CDs were used as controls and cells were cultured for 24 h. Thereafter, 20 μL of 5.0 mg/mL MTT was added to each well. The medium containing MTT was removed, and 150 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added. The resulting mixture was shaken at room temperature for about 10 min. Absorbance at 562 nm for each well was measured using a microplate reader. The cell viability is calculated as following.
Cell viability
%
=
OD treated
/
OD control
× 100 %
where ODcontrol and ODtreated were optical densities of cells in the absence and presence of N, P-CDs.

Samples containing buffer solution (PBS, 20 mM), different amounts of Sudan Red I and N, P-CDs were made up to 1.2 mL. The optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions, and the excitation wavelength (λex) was 430 nm, sit width was set at 10–20.

The samples were prepared with a standard addition method: appropriate amounts of Sudan Red I standard solution were added into the paprika powder commercially purchased from a local supermarket (Wuhan, China). Then 1.0 g prepared sample was extracted ultrasonically by 10 mL ethanol for 30 min and centrifugal sedimentation was carried out for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. At last, the extraction liquid was collected in a sealed flask for detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of N, P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This paper reports the preparation of yellow fluorescence emitting N, P-CDs (λem = 530 nm, QY = 14%) via a one-step process (60 °C, 30 min) using Glc and CA as carbon sources, and PEI1800 as a nitrogen source and H3PO4 as both phosphorus source and dehydrating agent.

Firstly, the optical properties of prepared N, P-CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 1a, an absorption peak exists at 360 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum, which is the result of n-π* transition from CO band [38] and N and P heteroatom induced excited defect state. Meanwhile, the emission wavelength is at 530 nm, under optimum excitation wavelength 430 nm (Fig. S1). The prepared CDs exhibit excitation dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 1b). Its emission peak shifts from 488 to 548 nm when excitation wavelength enhances from 320 to 500 nm, and fluorescence intensity reaches a maximum at λex = 430 nm. This excitation dependent behavior is attributed to the surface state of the gap band.

TEM image was used to further measure and characterize the structure and morphology of prepared N, P-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the prepared N, P-CDs are in spherical shape with uniform dispersion, and the average particle diameter is 12 nm.

To explore the surface state and the chemical components of prepared N, P-CDs, their XPS spectra were analyzed. The prepared N, P-CDs are composed by C, H, N, P and O elements, showing the existence of N and P co-doping. As shown in Fig. 3a, the XPS spectrum of N, P-CDs shows five peaks at 528.6, 397.7, 283.4, 191.5 and 131.3 eV, corresponding to the existence of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, P 2s and P 2p. The O1s part of the spectrum (Fig. 3b) shows the existence of C-OH, OP, *O=C-O and O=C-O* at the peaks of 530.9, 531.4, 531.9 and 532.6 eV, while the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows the existence of N-H，C-N-C and NC3 at the peaks of 399.2, 400.4 and 401.3 eV. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) of N, P-CDs shows four peaks at 284.4, 285.2, 286.1 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to the existence of C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O, C=C-N and CO groups; P 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) shows the existence of PN (133.1 eV) and PC (133.9 eV) groups. In addition, as shown in Table. S1, the contents of N and P in N, P-CDs reached 8.19% and 6.01% respectively, indicating that N and P were successfully doped into N, P-CDs.

The functional groups on the surface of N, P-CDs were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3f, the peak at 935 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching bond of CN, the peak at 1069 cm−1 is attributed to P-OR (R = C, N, P) stretching bond and the peak at 1307 cm−1 is the stretching bond of CN. Moreover, other characteristic peaks at 1476, 1570 and 1637 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching bonds of -CH2-, CN and CO. The broad absorption peak at 3450–2500 cm−1 is attributed to OH and NH, illustrating the abundant existence of hydroxyl and amino groups. Basically, these rich functional groups would endow charges of N, P-CDs, and improve its hydrophilic ability and stability in aqueous phase.

Most of present theories proposed that the doping of heteroatom is the main reason for the acquirement of high-quality N, P-CDs [9,11,17,24,39]. We proposed that the proper selection of nitrogen source, phosphorus source and carbon source would also impact the optical properties of prepared CDs. Therefore, the impacts of different carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus sources upon the optical properties of CDs were investigated (Fig. 4 ).

The experiments showed that the absence of any one of these three raw materials would only produce blue-emitting CDs with extremely low fluorescence intensities, showing the necessity of all three materials. First, to investigate the effect of carbon source (Glc) during the synthesis process, four other materials with similar structure unit to Glc, that is, hexanoic acid, starch, deoxyribose, sucrose, were used as carbon sources to produce CDs, and their optical properties were compared. As shown in Fig. 4a, when hexanoic acid works as carbon source, the fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs was extremely low. The only one -OH reacting site on hexanoic acid make it hard to dehydrate to form conjugate fluorophores. The number of -OH is an important factor for the synthesis of high-quality N, P-CDs. Deoxyribose could produce CDs with better fluorescence intensity, because deoxyribose is pentose with three -OH sites, which is prone to form the ring. However, the content of -OH in deoxyribose is still low, so the fluorescent intensity of prepared CDs is relatively weak.

As disaccharide, sucrose contains abundant -OH and its smaller particle structure is also beneficial to the doping of N and P elements, ensuring the high fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs. Starch is the polysaccharide conjugated by Glc, with more stable skeleton structure. Under low-temperature and in rapid reaction process, its macromolecule structure cannot sufficiently crack for the passivation process of PEI, making fluorescence of prepared CDs quite weak. Compared with above-mentioned four carbon sources, Glc has hexacarbonyl skeleton with multiple -OH groups, which makes the formation of aromatic structure quite easy. Its -OH groups would also oxidize and dehydrate under acidic and heated environment, and form conjugated structures like CO, making CDs emission wavelength redshift. Moreover, the addition amount of Glc into the system only impact the fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs, rather than the emission wavelength (Fig. S2a). Therefore, to acquire long-wavelength-emitting N, P-CDs under low temperature, the ideal carbon source should have proper skeleton structure and rich -OH groups as reaction sites for dehydration.

Proper phosphorus source with dehydrating, water absorbing and strong oxidizing abilities also plays a key role during the synthesis. Some common phosphorus compounds PPA, NaH2PO4 (acidic salt) and sodium pyrophosphate (basic salt) were selected to investigate the impact of phosphorus source upon the optical properties of prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the relatively long emission wavelengths of these prepared CDs show that the doping of P element could effectively prolong the emission wavelength of the product. The fluorescence intensities of CDs prepared by either NaH2PO4 or sodium pyrophosphate are all relatively poor, illustrating that the pH value is not the main factor that influence the optical quality of the products. The CDs prepared by PPA exhibit better fluorescence intensity than previous two compounds. Since PPA possesses water-absorbing ability, it proves that dehydrating ability of phosphorus source is beneficial to the detachment of -OH on carbon source to form conjugated fluorophore, and thus improves the fluorescence intensity of prepared N, P-CDs. The CDs prepared by H3PO4 exhibit the strongest fluorescence intensities. H3PO4 with both excellent dehydrating and oxidizing abilities could effectively condense the solution and make raw materials partial carbonization to form fluorophore, and thus enhance the fluorescence of prepared CDs. Moreover, the amount of H3PO4 could greatly influence the fluorescence intensity of the product. Excessive and inadequate addition amounts would lead to dramatic fluorescence intensity decrease (Fig. S2b).

PEI is a popular nitrogen source, and most CDs prepared with it all exhibit excellent optical properties, because of its abundant N content and special structure. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence intensities of two CDs prepared by EDA and PEI as nitrogen sources. The emission wavelengths of both acquired CDs are longer than 500 nm, showing that the doping of N element could effectively prolong the emission wavelengths of the products. Though the nitrogen content of EDA is higher than that of PEI, the mesh skeleton structure of PEI is more beneficial to the synthesis of CDs with much higher fluorescence intensity. To summarize, the carbon source Glc, nitrogen source PEI, and the phosphorus source H3PO4 all played multiple roles in determining the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of prepared CDs.

Short reaction time and relatively low reaction temperature are the typical features of this synthesis route. As shown in Fig. S2c, the length of reaction time does not influence the emission wavelength of prepared N, P-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity even drops with the increase of reaction time. Fig. S2d shows that the emission wavelength of prepared CDs redshifts with the rise of reaction temperature, and higher temperature (>60 °C) sets negative impact upon the fluorescence intensity. The short reaction time and low temperature conform to the concept of green chemistry, making the whole process fast and simple.

MIC is regarded as the gold standard to assess the antibacterial abilities of materials. As shown in Fig. S3a and b, N, P-CDs have significant inhibitory abilities to both E. coli and S. aureus with MIC at 0.5 and 7.5 μg·mL−1 respectively. This inhibition ability to S. aureus is much stronger than most antibacterial CDs [40,41]. For instance, Li and his team developed a degradable broad-spectrum antibacterial CDs, with best MIC to S. aureus at 100 μg·mL−1 [40]. The quaternized CDs reported by Zhao et al. could completely inhibit the growth of S. aureus at concentration of 20 μg·mL−1 [41]. The antibacterial ability of prepared N, P-CDs originates from the nitrogen source PEI. PEI is known as a cationic polymer with antibacterial ability [42–44]. The rich residue of PEI (−NH2) on the surface of prepared CDs because of low reaction temperature and short reaction time makes CDs inherit some biochemical functions of the raw materials.

To further investigate the influence of N, P-CDs on bacterial cells, the morphological changes of bacteria with treatment of N, P-CDs (0.5 mg·mL−1) for different time were observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 5a, S. aureus treated without N, P-CDs showed clear edges and smooth cell walls. After treated by N, P-CDs for 2 h, some cytoplasmic exudates from bacterial cells as shown in Fig. 5b. The cell surfaces became damaged and wrinkled, and the leakages of intracellular contents could be observed clearly for many cells after another 2 h (Fig. 5c). After incubation with N, P-CDs for 6 h, the cell wall of S. aureus shrunk or even completely collapsed and the leakages of cell contents increased (Fig. 5d), suggesting that N, P-CDs could be used in antibacterial application and the antimicrobial mechanism involved the disruption of bacterial cell walls and/or membranes.

The excellent antibacterial ability of N, P-CDs may be the result of electrostatic interaction between positively charged N, P-CDs and negatively charged bacteria. The zeta potential of N, P-CDs is +7.73 mV, because of the amino groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, whose existence has been proven by IR and XPS spectrum. N, P-CDs can be easily attracted by negatively-charged bacteria, and attach onto bacteria cells. CDs mechanically damage cell wall and penetrate into the cell, causing the cellular components leak and finally the death of bacterial cells.

Based on the studies of other researchers [26,45], we speculate that the antibacterial abilities of N, P-CDs to different bacteria might originate from the cell surface structure difference of bacteria. Gram-positive bacteria have thick but structurally simple cell walls similar to network structure, which is a peptidoglycan layer interconnected with the plasma membrane. The anionic sites of teichoic acid on the peptidoglycan layer promote the electrostatic binding of CDs onto the bacteria surface. For Gram-negative bacteria, they have a thin layer of glycan between the protective extracellular membrane and the cytoplasmic membrane, and the outer membrane is enriched with lipopolysaccharides, which are cross-bridged with divalent cations. The unique structure and composition of outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria make nanoparticles or hydrophobic molecules great difficult in attaching onto their surface or penetrate into the bacteria [45].

Excellent biocompatibility is the basic and key requirement to CDs before their application in cell imaging. To assess the biocompatibility of prepared CDs, the cytotoxicity of N, P-CDs was investigated. When the concentration of N, P-CDs was less than 150 μg·mL−1, the cell viability of HepG2 cells was more than 80% (Fig. S4).

The flexibility of multicolor fluorescence, ultra-small particle size and high biocompatibility of N, P-CDs promise it an ideal labeling agent in biological fields. S. aureus was used as bacterial model to test the biolabeling ability of CDs. A time-dependent bacterial labeling study was also performed (Fig. 6 ). The bacteria were cultivated with the treatment of N, P-CDs (10 mg·mL−1) for 1, 2, 3 h, respectively, and were observed under a confocal fluorescence microscope. Under different excitation wavelength, N, P-CDs labeled S. aureus emit blue (Fig. 6a, d and g), green (Fig. 6b, e and h), and red (Fig. 6c, f and i) color fluorescence. The bacteria emit very weak fluorescence after 1 h incubation with the presence of N, P-CDs. With the increase of incubation time, the fluorescence gets stronger. The N, P-CDs were able to retain their ability to fluoresce the cells after cellular uptake of 3 h of incubation and displayed excellent multicolor fluorescence. These results indicated that the multicolor N, P-CDs have exhibited very good biocompatibility and acted as nontoxic good candidates for fluorescence imaging of bacterial cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of Sudan Red I
ce:section.ce:para: In our study, the prepared N, P-CDs has been used as sensitive fluorescence probe to detect Sudan Red I. The quench ratios of multiple ions and other dyes to the fluorescence probe have been tested. As shown in Fig. 7 , the additions of 1 × 10−4 M metal ions and common anions have little or even no influence upon the fluorescence, while the addition of Sudan Red I of the same concentration makes the fluorescence almost completely quenched. Fig. S5 investigated the fluorescence stability of prepared N, P-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity stays stable even under UV light (λ = 365 nm) for 30 min. Since the optical properties of CDs can be easily influenced by its surface state, the impact of pH value of the system upon the fluorescence was examined. As shown in Fig. S6a, the fluorescence stays stable with pH ranging from 2 to 7. The quench ratio of fluorescence before and after the addition of Sudan Red I in different pH environments was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S6b, the quenching ratio is great when pH value is at 2 or 3, while it stays stable in the range of 4 to 7. Considering its future application in biological environment, the pH value for the system is then set at 6.

Fig. 8 exhibits the fluorescence spectra of N, P-CDs after the addition of different amounts of Sudan Red I (pH = 6). When the concentration of Sudan Red I rise from 43 nM to 52.17 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with the obvious redshifts of emission wavelength (from 525 to 556 nm). The quenching behavior follows Stern-Volmer equation:

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of Sudan Red I, Ksv is the quenching constant (7.42 × 105 L·mol−1), which is calculated through fitting curve equation, and [c] is the concentration of Sudan Red I. The calculation shows that the concentration of Sudan Red I keeps excellent linear relationship to the fluorescence quenching ratio with detection range from 43 nM to 52 μM, correlation coefficient R2 at 0.9978 and limit of detection (LOD) at 43 nM. Compared with some reported studies [30,46–49], the detection method of using N, P-CDs as a fluorescent probe is easier and more sensitive (Table S2).

To investigate the quenching mechanism of Sudan Red I to the fluorescence of N, P-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Sudan Red I and the emission spectra of N, P-CDs are compared. As shown in Fig. 9 , Sudan Red I show broad absorption peak at 300–350 nm, which greatly overlaps with the emission spectra (530 nm) of N, P-CDs, rendering Sudan Red I's effective screening of emission of N, P-CDs. This proves that the quenching behavior of Sudan Red I is caused by internal filter effect (IFE), which is also the reason for the redshifts of emission wavelength during the quenching. In the absence and presence of Sudan red I, the average fluorescence lifetimes of N, P-CDs were 3.24 ns and 4.19 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the N, P-CDs had almost no change. This suggested that no obvious electron transfer or significant excited-state interaction between N, P-CDs and Sudan red I acting in the sensing process, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching of N, P-CDs by Sudan red I is attributed to IFE rather than other possible mechanisms [50–52]. With the addition of Sudan Red I, whose absorption peak overlaps with the first part (450–550 nm) of emission peak of N, P-CDs, the fluorescence was more quenched at the front part of the spectra than the back part, which explains the redshift of emission peak during the quenching process.

As an azo dye, Sudan Red is often used in food dyeing by unscrupulous peddlers [53]. The N, P-CDs-based probing platform to Sudan Red I has been applied to the detection in paprika of food samples. Table 1 shows the detection result and recovery rate of the system. After adding two different concentrations of Sudan Red I to the tested solution, the recovery rates are 90.8% and 97.7%, respectively, with the relative standard deviation after three repeated measurement at 8.18% and 6.22%.

This paper reports the rapid synthesis of high-quality, yellow-emitting N, P-CDs (λem = 530 nm) under low reaction temperature, with Glc as carbon source, PEI as nitrogen source and H3PO4 as a phosphorus source. The impacts of raw materials upon the optical properties of prepared N, P-CDs have been systematically investigated, offering reference for the selection of raw materials in the future synthesis. The prepared CDs have been characterized through multiple means, and its synthesis and luminescence mechanisms have been discussed and proposed. Meanwhile, the prepared N, P-CDs exhibit strong inhibitory ability to bacteria, and the antibacterial mechanism was also studied. The CDs have also been used to realize fluorescence imaging of S. aureus. Based on the internal filter effect between N, P-CDs and azo dye Sudan Red I, the fluorescence probe to Sudan Red I has been developed, and the sensitive detection of Sudan Red I in real food samples has been realized.
Dan Zhao: conceptualization, methodology, project administration, supervision, writing - original draft, writing - review & editing.
Zhixia Zhang: resources, data curation, formal analysis, validation, visualization.
Xuemei Liu: resources, data curation, formal analysis, validation, visualization.
Rui Zhang: resources, data curation, formal analysis, validation, visualization.
Xincai Xiao: conceptualization, methodology, formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province(2016CFB615), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, South Central University for Nationalities (CZY15020, CZY19029, CZW15017). The research was funded by the China Scholarship Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111468.
